step_by_step_thinking: I agree with the selection of Option B, cyclophosphamide. While both cyclophosphamide and intravenous pulse corticosteroids could potentially manage the nephrotic syndrome, cyclophosphamide offers several advantages particularly suited to this case due to its ability to suppress immune responses involved in glomerulonephritis without the immediate rebound effect seen after cessation of high-dose steroids. Additionally, it carries fewer acute cardiovascular risks than frequent IV corticosteroid administration. Therefore, my final answer remains B: Cyclophosphamide.
raw_response:
I agree with the selection of Option B, cyclophosphamide. While both cyclophosphamide and intravenous pulse corticosteroids could potentially manage the nephrotic syndrome, cyclophosphamide offers several advantages particularly suited to this case due to its ability to suppress immune responses involved in glomerulonephritis without the immediate rebound effect seen after cessation of high-dose steroids. Additionally, it carries fewer acute cardiovascular risks than frequent IV corticosteroid administration. Therefore, my final answer remains B: Cyclophosphamide. 

Answer: B.
